name: googlenet

members.name: GoogLeNetConfig
members.signature: GoogLeNetConfig(num_classes: int = 1000, in_channels: int = 3, aux_logits: bool = True, dropout: float = 0.4, aux_dropout: float = 0.7)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(num_classes: int = 1000, in_channels: int = 3, aux_logits: bool = True, dropout: float = 0.4, aux_dropout: float = 0.7)

members.extended:
`aux_logits` enables the two auxiliary classifiers that were used
during training in the original paper.  They are ignored at inference
(eval mode); the auxiliary loss is typically weighted 0.3 in the total
training objective.

`dropout` — main classifier dropout rate (0.4 in the paper).
`aux_dropout` — auxiliary classifier dropout rate (0.7 in the paper).

members.name: GoogLeNet
members.extended:
Implements the 22-layer Inception v1 network from Szegedy et al.,
"Going Deeper with Convolutions", CVPR 2015: a five-block stem
(Conv $7\times7$ stride-2 → MaxPool → $1\times1$ →
$3\times3$ → MaxPool) followed by nine
`_InceptionModule` blocks grouped into three stages
(Inception 3a-3b → MaxPool → 4a-4e → MaxPool → 5a-5b) and a final
`lucid.nn.AdaptiveAvgPool2d` to a $1\times1$ spatial
map.  Every convolution is batch-normalised, matching the canonical
distributed ImageNet checkpoint.  The two auxiliary classifiers
attached at Inception 4a and 4d in the original paper are part of
the classifier variant only, not the backbone.

parameters.name: config
parameters.description:
Frozen architecture spec.  Use `googlenet` for the
paper-cited configuration; pass a custom config to switch input
channel count.  `aux_logits` is irrelevant for the backbone.

examples:
Build a GoogLeNet backbone and run a single forward pass:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.googlenet import googlenet
>>> backbone = googlenet()
>>> x = lucid.randn(2, 3, 224, 224)
>>> out = backbone(x)
>>> out.logits.shape   # (B, 1024, 1, 1)
(2, 1024, 1, 1)
notes:
Each `_InceptionModule` computes four parallel branches in a
single layer:



$$
y = \mathrm{concat}\bigl(
    f_{1\times1}(x),\; f_{3\times3}(g_{1\times1}(x)),\;
    f_{3\times3}(h_{1\times1}(x)),\;
    p_{1\times1}(\mathrm{pool}(x))
\bigr).
$$

The $1\times1$ bottlenecks before the larger spatial
convolutions keep the parameter count manageable: GoogLeNet has
only ≈6.8 M parameters compared with ≈60 M for AlexNet, despite
being substantially deeper and reaching a top-5 ImageNet validation
error of 6.67%.  This was the ILSVRC-2014 classification winner.

attributes.name: config
attributes.description: Stored copy of the config that built this model.

attributes.name: inception3a, inception3b
attributes.description:
First Inception stage, producing a $28\times28$ spatial
map with 256 and 480 channels respectively.

attributes.name: inception4a … inception4e
attributes.description:
Second Inception stage at $14\times14$ resolution; widths
ramp 512 → 512 → 512 → 528 → 832.

attributes.name: inception5a, inception5b
attributes.description:
Third Inception stage at $7\times7$ resolution, producing
the final 1024-channel feature map.

attributes.name: maxpool3, maxpool4
attributes.description:
Ceil-mode max-pools between Inception stages
($3\times3$ stride-2 and $2\times2$ stride-2).

attributes.name: avgpool
attributes.description:
Final global average pool collapsing the $7\times7$
feature map to $1\times1$.

attributes.name: feature_info
attributes.description:
Per-stage descriptor (channels + reduction factor) exposed via
`BackboneMixin`.

members.name: GoogLeNetForImageClassification

methods.name: forward
methods.signature: forward(x: Tensor, labels: Tensor | None = None)

members.summary: GoogLeNet image classifier with optional auxiliary classifiers.
members.extended:
Combines the GoogLeNet backbone with a global-average-pool +
`lucid.nn.Dropout` (`config.dropout`, default 0.4) +
`lucid.nn.Linear` head producing `config.num_classes`
logits, plus two optional auxiliary classifiers attached at
Inception 4a (512-channel feature map) and Inception 4d
(528-channel feature map).  Auxiliary heads are evaluated only in
training mode and only when `config.aux_logits=True`; their
contributions to the loss are weighted 0.3 each, following the
original paper.

parameters.name: config
parameters.description:
Architecture spec.  Use `googlenet_cls` for the
paper-cited configuration (auxiliary heads enabled).  Pass
`aux_logits=False` for an inference-only variant.

examples:
Inference path (no labels, auxiliary heads inactive):
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.googlenet import googlenet_cls
>>> model = googlenet_cls().eval()
>>> x = lucid.randn(4, 3, 224, 224)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.logits.shape
(4, 1000)
>>> out.aux_logits1 is None
True
Training path (auxiliary heads active, labels provided):
>>> model = model.train()
>>> labels = lucid.tensor([0, 1, 2, 3], dtype=lucid.int64)
>>> out = model(x, labels=labels)
>>> out.aux_logits1.shape   # available during training
(4, 1000)
notes:
From Szegedy et al., "Going Deeper with Convolutions", CVPR 2015,
§5.  The full training loss with auxiliary heads is



$$
\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{main}}
    + 0.3 \cdot \mathcal{L}_{\text{aux1}}
    + 0.3 \cdot \mathcal{L}_{\text{aux2}},
$$

each term being the standard categorical cross-entropy
$-\frac{1}{N} \sum_n \log \operatorname{softmax}(\cdot)_{y_n}$.
The $0.3$ weighting compensates for the auxiliary heads being
much shallower than the main classifier; at inference only the main
branch is evaluated.

attributes.name: config
attributes.description: Stored copy of the config that built this model.

attributes.name: conv1, conv2, conv3, maxpool1, maxpool2, inception3a, ...,
drop
attributes.description:
Dropout applied before the main classifier
(`p=config.dropout`, 0.4 in the paper).

attributes.name: aux1, aux2
attributes.description:
Auxiliary classifiers (instances of `_AuxClassifier`)
when `config.aux_logits=True`; otherwise
`lucid.nn.Identity` placeholders.

members.name: GoogLeNetOutput
members.signature: GoogLeNetOutput(logits: Tensor, aux_logits1: Tensor | None = None, aux_logits2: Tensor | None = None, loss: Tensor | None = None)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(logits: Tensor, aux_logits1: Tensor | None = None, aux_logits2: Tensor | None = None, loss: Tensor | None = None)

members.extended:
Carries the main classifier logits plus the optional auxiliary
classifier logits emitted during training.  Auxiliary heads attach
at Inception 4a and 4d in the original paper and are evaluated
*only* in training mode when `config.aux_logits=True`; at
inference both auxiliary fields are `None`.

parameters.name: logits
parameters.description: Main classifier output of shape `(B, num_classes)`.

parameters.name: aux_logits1
parameters.description:
Logits from the first auxiliary classifier (attached at
Inception 4a) of shape `(B, num_classes)`; `None` at
inference or when `aux_logits=False`.

parameters.name: aux_logits2
parameters.description:
Logits from the second auxiliary classifier (attached at
Inception 4d) of shape `(B, num_classes)`; `None` at
inference or when `aux_logits=False`.

parameters.name: loss

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.googlenet import googlenet_cls
>>> model = googlenet_cls().eval()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.logits.shape
(1, 1000)
>>> out.aux_logits1 is None
True
notes:
From Szegedy et al., "Going Deeper with Convolutions", CVPR 2015,
§5.  When auxiliary heads are active the training loss combines
the main and auxiliary cross-entropies as



$$
\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{main}}
    + 0.3 \cdot \mathcal{L}_{\text{aux1}}
    + 0.3 \cdot \mathcal{L}_{\text{aux2}},
$$

a weighting taken directly from the paper that compensates for the
auxiliary heads' shallower position in the network.

members.name: googlenet
members.extended:
Builds a `GoogLeNet` with the paper-cited Szegedy 2015
topology: a Conv-MaxPool stem followed by nine
`_InceptionModule` blocks at three resolutions
(28×28 → 14×14 → 7×7) and a final
`lucid.nn.AdaptiveAvgPool2d` to $1\times1$.
Approximately 6.8 M parameters in the backbone — roughly **12×
fewer** than AlexNet despite being substantially deeper.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
Reserved for future pretrained-weight loading.  Currently
ignored — the returned model is randomly initialised.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description:
Keyword overrides forwarded into `GoogLeNetConfig`
(`in_channels`, etc.).  Auxiliary-classifier fields are
irrelevant for the backbone.

returns.description:
Backbone with the GoogLeNet configuration applied (or with
`overrides` merged on top of it).

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.googlenet import googlenet
>>> model = googlenet()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.logits.shape   # (B, 1024, 1, 1)
(1, 1024, 1, 1)
notes:
See Szegedy et al., "Going Deeper with Convolutions", CVPR 2015 —
the ILSVRC-2014 classification winner with a top-5 ImageNet
validation error of 6.67%.  Single architecture; no paper-cited
"tiny / large" variants (H11).

members.name: googlenet_cls
members.signature: googlenet_cls(pretrained: bool | str = False, weights: GoogLeNetWeights | None = None, overrides: object = {})
members.summary: GoogLeNet (Inception v1) image classifier with auxiliary heads.
members.extended:
Builds a `GoogLeNetForImageClassification` with the
paper-cited Szegedy 2015 configuration: 22-layer Inception backbone
+ global-average-pool + dropout (`p=0.4`) + linear projection to
`config.num_classes`, plus two auxiliary classifiers attached at
Inception 4a and 4d (enabled by default via `aux_logits=True`).
Approximately 13.0 M parameters total when auxiliary heads are
included.  Reaches **69.778% top-1 on ImageNet-1k**.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
Pretrained-weight selector.  `False` → random init; `True`
→ the `DEFAULT` tag (`GoogLeNetWeights.IMAGENET1K_V1`);
a tag string → that specific checkpoint.  Mutually exclusive
with `weights` (which wins if both are given).

parameters.name: weights
parameters.description:
Explicit weights enum member.  Takes precedence over
`pretrained`.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description:
Keyword overrides forwarded into `GoogLeNetConfig`.  Use
`aux_logits=False` to disable auxiliary classifiers for
cheaper inference graphs, `num_classes=N` to retarget the
head, or `dropout=p` / `aux_dropout=p` to adjust
regularisation.

returns.description:
Classifier with the GoogLeNet configuration applied (or with
`overrides` merged on top of it).

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.googlenet import googlenet_cls
>>> model = googlenet_cls().eval()
>>> x = lucid.randn(2, 3, 224, 224)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.logits.shape
(2, 1000)
notes:
See Szegedy et al., "Going Deeper with Convolutions", CVPR 2015,
§5.  Auxiliary classifiers were introduced specifically to combat
vanishing gradients in this 22-layer network without the benefit of
residual connections; they contribute to the loss with weight
$0.3$ each during training and are discarded at inference.
Pretrained weights are converted from torchvision's
`GoogLeNet_Weights.IMAGENET1K_V1` and hosted under
`lucid-dl/googlenet`.

members.name: GoogLeNetWeights
members.extended:
Single ImageNet-1k checkpoint converted from torchvision's
`GoogLeNet_Weights.IMAGENET1K_V1` (Szegedy et al., 2015; ~13.0M
params with auxiliary heads, 69.778% top-1).

summary: GoogLeNet (Inception v1) family — Szegedy et al., 2014.
citation: Szegedy, Christian, et al. "Going Deeper with Convolutions." Proceedings of the IEEE Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR), 2015, pp. 1–9.
theory:
GoogLeNet (Inception v1) is the ILSVRC-2014 classification winner
and introduced the *Inception module* — a multi-branch building
block that lets a single layer apply convolutions at several
receptive-field scales in parallel and concatenate their outputs.
The motivation is that natural images contain salient structure at
multiple sizes, and forcing the network to commit to a single
kernel per layer wastes capacity.

Each Inception module computes, in parallel, a $1\times1$
convolution, a $3\times3$ convolution, a $5\times5$
convolution, and a $3\times3$ max-pool branch.  To keep the
computation tractable, *cheap* $1\times1$ bottlenecks are
placed before the larger spatial convolutions and after the
pooling branch.  Concatenating along the channel axis gives the
next layer an enriched, multi-scale feature representation.
Formally, an Inception block produces



$$
y = \mathrm{concat}\bigl(
    f_{1\times1}(x),\; f_{3\times3}(g_{1\times1}(x)),\;
    f_{5\times5}(h_{1\times1}(x)),\; p_{1\times1}(\mathrm{pool}(x))
\bigr),
$$

where each $f$ is a learned convolution and each
$g, h, p_{1\times1}$ is a dimensionality-reducing
$1\times1$ projection.

GoogLeNet stacks nine such modules into a 22-layer network with
only 5 M parameters — roughly **12×** fewer than AlexNet — while
achieving a top-5 error of 6.67%.  Two auxiliary classifiers were
attached to intermediate layers during training to combat
vanishing gradients and add a regularising effect; they are
discarded at inference.  The Inception family launched here led to
Inception v2/v3 (factorised convolutions, label smoothing) and
eventually Inception-ResNet.
